Facts.text: Arabs gain control of most of the Region of Palestine from the Byzantine Empire
John V begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Frederick II is crowned King of the Romans (King of the Germans) in Aachen
Rindfleisch ("Beef") persecutions - Jewish community in Würzburg, Germany, is massacred
Turkey recognizes John Sigismund Zápolya as vassal king of Hungary
Battle of Grevelingen: Lamoraal, Count of Egmont defeats France
Dutch city Groningen held by Spain surrenders to Dutch and England army led by Maurice of Orange
Italian painter Caravaggio gets his first public commission, the Contarelli Chapel in the church of San Luigi dei Francesi, Rome
Three hundred colonists bound for New France depart from Dieppe, France
Four British ships arrive in Boston to drive the Dutch out of New York
Treaty of Pruth is signed, ending the Russo-Turkish War (1710-11), and Azov port is returned to Ottoman control
James Otis publishes "The Rights of the British Colonies Asserted and Proved," in which he argues against taxation without representation
The Prussians conquer Mainz
Republican Robert Emmet leads Irish rebellion against the British Crown in Dublin
Battle of Mogilev: first significant fighting duringNapoleon's invasion of Russia results in minor French victory
First US swimming school opens in Boston, Massachusetts
Cornerstones are laid for the construction of thee Mormon Kirtland Temple in Kirtland, Ohio
HMS Beagle anchors in Bay of Valparaiso (Chile)
Union Act passed by the British Parliament, uniting Upper and Lower Canada
Battle of Custoza during the Italian War of Independence starts
17th Postmaster General: Nathan K. Hall of New York takes office
Treaty of Traverse des Sioux signed by Sioux Indians and US
Private John Brown becomes the first interment in the National Cemetery at the Presidio in San Francisco, California
Jewish Disabilities Removal Act passes in British Parliament
President Lincoln names Army of the Potomac General Henry Halleck as US Army General-in-Chief
Alexandra Park opens in North London, England
Cincinnati Baseball Club (Red Stockings) forms
All England Lawn Tennis Club is founded as the All England Croquet Club; in 1877, the name changes to the All England Croquet and Lawn Tennis Club
Aires de Ornelas e Vasconcelos is appointed Archbishop of the Portuguese colonial enclave of Goa
First telephone and telegraph line in Hawaii is completed
The first US municipal railroad, Cincinnati Southern, begins operations
American civilian Steve Brodie allegedly survives a 135-foot plunge from the Brooklyn Bridge
John Boyd Dunlop applies for a patent for the pneumatic tyre
First Pan-African Congress meets in London, organized by Trinidadian Henry Sylvester Williams, makes plea for African self-government
The Canadian government reviews immigration policy, prohibiting criminals and paupers from landing in Canada
Dutch Excelsior soccer club is established in Rotterdam (First Division winners 1973-4, 78-79, 2005-06)
Ice cream cone is created during the St. Louis World's Fair, reputedly first by Charles E. Menches
Pogroms against Jews in Odessa, Russia
Arabs attack Jewish community of Rechovot, Palestine
Austria-Hungary presents an ultimatum to Serbia, demanding acceptance within 48 hours, making peacekeeping difficult
Cleveland Metropolitan Park District established in Greater Cleveland, Ohio
British East Africa is renamed Kenya and becomes a British Crown colony
American long jumper Edward Gourdin sets new world record of 7.69 m (25 ft 2+3⁄4 in) in Cambridge, Massachusetts

Chen Duxiu, Li Dazhao and others found the Chinese Communist Party in Shanghai, with guidance from the Soviet-backed Communist International (Comintern) and Dutch communist Henk Sneevliet. Chen Duxiu is elected its leader.
National Railway Company of Belgium forms (NMBS/SNCFB)
The Fascist government in Italy bans the use of foreign words
Earthquake strikes Ariano in Italy, killing 1,500
Pitts Pie Traynor ends both games with home runs (first in 9th, second in 13th)
France announces it can't afford to send a team to the 1932 Los Angeles Olympics due to the Great Depression but eventually manages to send a team
The United Kingdom transfers the Ashmore and Cartier Islands in the Indian Ocean to Australia
Isolation of pituitary hormone is announced (Yale University)
German troops conquer Rostow
World War II: Operation Edelweiss (a German plan to gain control over the Caucasus) begins
The US 45th Infantry Division occupies the north coast of Termini, Sicily
Bretton Woods Conference ends, establishes the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development, later part of the World Bank, and the International Monetary Fund
Chicago Cubs' Bill Nicholson hits four home runs in a doubleheader
Soviet Army marches into Lublin, Poland
US forces invade Japanese-held Tinian in WWII
US troops occupy Pisa, Italy
First US Navy air squadron of jets, Quonset Point, Rhode Island
Progressive party convention nominates Henry Wallace for US President
Test cricket debut of England's Brian Close at age 18 years and 149 days
General Naguib seizes power and overthrows the monarchy in Egypt (National Day)
English speed ace Donald Campbell drives Bluebird K7 to a new water speed record at Ullswater in the English Lake District, becoming the first boat to surpass 200 mph at 202.32 mph (325.60 km/h)
NY Yankees Bob Cerv and E Howard are first to hit consecutive pinch home runs
Bell X-2 rocket plane sets world aircraft speed record of 3,050 km/h
The Loi Cadre is passed by the French Republic to organize French overseas territory affairs
First four women named to peerage in House of Lords (UK)
The International Agreement on the Neutrality of Laos is signed
Egyptian munition ship "Star of Alexandria" explodes at dockside in Bone, Algeria; 100 die, 160 are injured, $20 million in damage
Dick Stuart hits a home run in a Major League record 23rd different park
The Beatles' single "Help" is released in the UK
Cavern Club in Liverpool reopens
John Pennel pole vaults a record of 5.34 meters
Napoleon XIV releases "They're Coming to Take Me Away, Ha! Ha!"
43 die in a race riot in Detroit (2,000 injured, 442 fires)
First successful liver transplant on 19-month-old Julie Rodriguez by Dr Starzl at the University of Colorado
Pirate radio station Swinging Scotland closes down for financial reasons
Fred Blasie wins fifth wrestling world championship belt
PLO's first hijacking of an El Al plane
Race riot in Cleveland kills 11 people, including 3 police officers
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
A ban on parades and public processions until January 1971 is announced by the Stormont government (Northern Ireland Parliament)
Qaboos bin Said al Said becomes Sultan of Oman after overthrowing his father, Sa'id ibn Taimur
The British Army carries out early morning raids across Northern Ireland and arrests 48 people
First Earth Resources Technology Satellite (ERTS) is launched to document global land use change as a joint NASA/USGS program and is later renamed Landsat 1
Ozark AL Flight 809 is knocked out of the air by lightning while landing in St. Louis, killing 38
US PresidentRichard Nixonrefuses to release Watergate tapes of conversations in the White House relevant to the Watergate investigation
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Greek military dictatorship collapses
LA Dodgers W. Crawford and Lee Lacy are 5th to hit consecutive pinch HRs
42nd NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Pittsburgh 24, All-Stars 0 (52,895 attendees)
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Wings release single "Let 'em In"
Washington jury convicts 12 Hanafi Muslims on hostage charges
Franklin Bradshaw is murdered in Salt Lake City by his grandson Marc Schreuder at the instigation of his mother Frances Schreuder
Israeli government rejects Sadat's call for the return of two Sinai areas
Mayence returns to helps Yankees win their fifth consecutive game
Soyuz 37 ferries two cosmonauts, including one Vietnamese, to Salyut 6
FCC approves AM stereo radio; KTSA in San Antonio, Texas, becomes the first station in the US to broadcast in stereo
International Whaling Commission votes for a total ban on commercial whaling starting in 1985
Air Canada Flight 143 runs out of fuel and makes a successful deadstick landing at Gimli, Manitoba, earning the nickname "Gimli Glider"
Petra Felke (East Germany) throws javelin 78.89 m (women's record)
RNI (Brooklyn, New York pirate radio station) begins broadcasting on 1620 AM
Said Aouita of Morocco runs world record 5,000 m (12:58.39)
Saskatchewan's Dave Ridgway kicks record eight field goals vs. Edmonton
Fox-TV tops ABC, NBC, and CBS for the first time with America's Most Wanted
Winds gust to 85 MPH at Fort Smith, Arkansas
Grateful Dead keyboardist Brent Mydland performs unexpected last show with the group at the World Music Theater, Tinley Park, Illinois
Rosie Jones (St. Louis), 26, crowned 22nd Miss Black America
South African workers' union leader Billy Nair (60) is arrested on treason charges but is released after suffering a heart attack
James Farentino of "Dynasty" arrested in Canada for cocaine possession
Rob Dibble, back from a 3-game suspension, is ejected for throwing at and hitting Cubs baserunner Doug Dascenzo as the Cubs win 8-5
China Northwest Airlines BAe146-300 crashes at Yinchuan, killing 55
Chris Boardman cycles a world-record 1-hour distance of 52.27 km
NY Yankee Mark Hutton becomes the first Australian to be a starting pitcher as they beat the Angels 5-2; Yankees, Red Sox, and Blue Jays are in a three-way tie for first
All 40,000 ceiling tiles in the Kingdome must be replaced
Amy Osmond of Utah, 17, crowned America's Junior Miss
Goodwill Games III open in St. Petersburg, Russia
Space Shuttle STS-65 Columbia 17 lands after a record 14 days and 55 minutes
Comet Hale-Bopp is discovered and becomes visible to the naked eye nearly a year later
Dallas TV Station KXAS settle with Dallas Cowboys Michael Irvin and Erik Williams for reporting false sex assault allegations against them
The US State Department rules that Turkey's agreement to purchase $23 billion worth of natural gas from Iran does not violate the Iran and Libya Sanctions Act
ANA Flight 61 is hijacked in Tokyo, Japan
Crown Prince Mohammed Ben Al-Hassan is crowned King Mohammed VI of Morocco on the death of his father
Three bombs explode in the Naama Bay area of Sharm el-Sheikh, Egypt, killing 88 people
Cape Verde joins the World Trade Organization, becoming its 153rd member
Mark Buehrle of the Chicago White Sox becomes the 18th pitcher to throw a perfect game in MLB history, defeating Tampa Bay Rays 5-0
The Bank of Canada announces the end of the recession even though it remains nascent and still dependent on government stimulus money
A series of attacks across Iraq kill 103 people
19 people are killed and 22 are injured after a tour bus collides with a truck in Saraburi, Thailand
20th Commonwealth Games open in Glasgow, Scotland
TransAsia Airways Flight 222 crashes into buildings in Taiwan, killing 44 people
NASA's Kepler mission announces the discovery of the most Earth-like planet yet, Kepler-452b, located 1,400 light-years from Earth
Suicide bomb in Kabul kills 80 during a protest march; Islamic State claims responsibility
"Sabrina" by Nick Drnaso becomes the first graphic novel named to the longlist for Man Booker Prize for best fiction written in English
250,000 doses of vaccines for children produced by a firm in China are faulty, according to reports in China
Dam collapses while under construction in Attapeu province, Laos, killing at least 20 with more than 100 missing
Environmental reports find China on track to reach new emission targets, but carbon dioxide emissions rise to 9.2 billion metric tons in 2017, more than the US and Europe combined
International Monetary Fund predicts inflation of one million percent in Venezuela by end of 2018
Japan records its highest-ever temperature at 41.1°C (105.98°F) in Kumagaya
Slum homes for 30,000 people demolished to make way for a dual carriage way in Nairobi, Kenya
At least 36 people killed by a mud landslide in Shuicheng county, Guizhou province, China
BHP becomes the first mining company to tackle pollution and says it will invest $400 million to reduce emissions
Investigation launched after no girls born in three months in 132 villages in Uttarkashi district, India, with sex-selective abortions suspected as the reason
Nike's Jordan Brand signs 2019 NBA #1 draft pick Zion Williamson to richest multiyear sponsorship deal for a rookie in history; estimated 7 years for $75 million
China launches its first mission to Mars, Tianwen-1, a combined orbiter, lander, and rover, from Wenchang Launch Site, Hainan Island
South Africa records more than 400,000 COVID-19 cases amid surging infections and decides to shut schools for a month
US confirmed cases of COVID-19 surpass 4 million with a death toll over 143,000; the real number of cases is likely up to 13 times higher according to the CDC
At least 164 people die in floods and landslides caused by heavy rain in Indian state of Maharashtra
Indonesia posts a record daily number of COVID-19 deaths at 1,566
MLB Cleveland Indians announce team will be renamed the Guardians after the Guardians of Traffic, eight large Art Deco statues on the Hope Memorial Bridge located near their playing field
XXXII Summer Olympic Games officially open at the Olympic Stadium, Tokyo, Japan
WHO declares monkeypox to be a public health emergency of international concern with 16,000 cases in 75 countries
Greece mounts it biggest evacuation, moving 19,000 tourists and residents out of the path of wildfires on the island of Rhodes amid the country's hottest July in 50 years
Prime Minister Hun Sen Cambodian People’s Party (CPP) declares a landslide win in the country's election, amid widespread criticism after any credible opponents were barred from running
Scott Rolen and Fred McGriff are inducted into Baseball Hall of Fame, Cooperstown, New York
Snap election in Spain fails to produce a majority government with conservative People's Party winning most seats
China announces plans to raise its retirement age from 60 for men and 55/50 for women, one of the lowest in the world
US Secret Service Director Kimberly Cheatle steps down after the agency failed to prevent an assassination attempt onDonald Trump
Idaho killer Bryan Kohberger is sentenced for four consecutive life sentences in prison without parole after pleading guilty to murdering four University of Idaho students in 2022
Jews are expelled from Vienne, France, by order of PopeInnocent IV
Battle of Gavere:Philip the Gooddefeats Ghent rebellion
EmperorCharles Vand the Schmalkaldic League sign Peace of Nuremberg
William I of Orange's troops occupy Roermond from the Spanish
Francis Drakedeparts his land-claim of Nova Albion, north of present day San Francisco, to cross the Pacific Oceana board the 'Golden Hind'
Benjamin Franklinsails back to Philadelphia
Charles Edward Stuart, "the Young Pretender" orBonnie Prince Charlie, lands on Eriskay Island in the Hebrides, beginning the final Jacobite Rebellion
KingLouis XVIof France and his Foreign Minister clandestinely agree to supply the United States with munitions during the American Revolution
Polish military leaderCasimir Pulaskiarrives in Marblehead, Massachusetts, to volunteer in the Continental Army cavalry
Alexandre de Beauharnais, the first husband ofJoséphine de Beauharnais, is executed for treason during the Reign of Terror
Napoleon Bonapartecaptures Alexandria, Egypt
EmperorNapoleon IIIappoints Empress Eugénie de Montijo as Regent of France
African American inventorElijah McCoyis granted a patent for lubricators for steam engines
NY YankeeLou Gehrighits his first of 23 career grand slams
French government ofRaymond Poincaréforms
Adolf Hitler's Directive number 45: order for army to advance on Stalingrad
MarshalPhilippe Pétain, leader of the French Vichy collaborationist regime during World War II, goes on trial
Joe CroninandHank Greenbergare inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, New York
US Vice PresidentRichard Nixonbegins visit to the USSR
American opera singerGrace Bumbrybecomes the first Black singer to perform at the Bayreuth Festival in Germany, earning 42 curtain calls
Kansas City A'sBert Campanerishits a home run on the first pitch he sees from Twins' Jim Kaat and adds a second home run later, also off Kaat in Minnesota
Frank Sinatra's album "Strangers in the Night" hits #1 on the US charts and wins a Grammy for Record of the Year and Best Male Vocal Performance
Alan Ayckbourn's play "Absent Friends" premieres in London
Baltimore OrioleReggie Jacksonhomers in six consecutive games
MLB Philadelphia Phillies'Steve Carltonbecomes the 78th MLB pitcher to win 200 games
George Brettof the Kansas City Royals achieves his 1,000th career hit in Major League Baseball
River of No Return Wilderness Area, surrounding the Salmon River in Idaho, designated by US PresidentJimmy Carter
KC RoyalsDan Quisenberryachieves his 200th career save
Moroccan KingHassan IImeets with Israeli Prime MinisterShimon Peres
Ringo Starr's first All-Starr Band debuts in concert; members include Joe Walsh,Nils Lofgren, Rick Danko, Levon Helm, Clarence Clemons, Dr. John,Billy Preston, and Jim Keltner
Bruce Springsteenbegins a world tour, his first with musicians other than the E Street Band
American dancer, actor, and directorGene Kellysuffers a mild stroke
Don Mattinglybecomes the sixth New York Yankee to get 2,000 hits
British Open Men's Golf, St Andrews:Tiger Woodsbeats Thomas Bjørn andErnie Elsby eight shots to win his first Open title and becomes the youngest player at only 24 to win all four major titles
English boy band "One Direction" is formed during the "X Factor" television talent show as Niall Horan,Harry Styles,Liam Payne,Zayn Malik, andLouis Tomlinsonjoin together
Supreme Court rejectsBill Cosby's petition against a civil case of his alleged sexual assault of 15-year-old girl at the Playboy Mansion in 1974
Boris Johnsonis chosen the new British Prime Minister by the ruling Conservative Party to replace Theresa May
USPresident Trumpsays he could send 75,000 federal agents to deal with violence in American cities like Portland, drawing backlash
William Austin Burt patents America's first typewriter, the typographer
US Senate passes bill championed by comedianJon Stewartto ensure 9/11 first responders fund never runs out of money
Yazid I [Yazīd ibn Mu‘āwiya Lanatullah], second Umayyad caliph (680–683), born in Arabia (d. 683)
Otto, Duke of Austria and Styria, born in Vienna (d. 1339)
Louis I, Duke of Anjou (1360-84) and King of Naples (Battle at Poitiers), born in Château de Vincennes, France (d. 1384)
Francesco Sforza, Italian condottiero and Duke of Milan (1401–1466), born in San Miniato, Tuscany (d. 1466)
Anna of Bohemia and Hungary, Queen of the Romans, born in Buda, Hungary (d. 1547)
Clement XI [Giovanni F Albani], Italian pope (ruled 1700-21), born in Urbino, Papal States (d. 1721)
Antonius Schultingh [Schultingius], Dutch lawyer, born in Nijmegen, Netherlands (d. 1734)
Francis Blomefield, English topographer (History of Norfolk), born in Fersfield, Norfolk (d. 1752)
Johannes Herbst, German-American musician, composer, and Moravian minister, born in Kempten, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1812)[1]
Alexey Titov, Russian composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1827)
Karl Ludwig Hellwig, German composer, born in Bliesdorf, Margraviate of Brandenburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1838)
Thomas Brisbane, British soldier, administrator and astronomer, born in Ayrshire, Scotland (d. 1860)
Étienne-Louis Malus, French physicist (Polarization of light), born in Paris (d. 1812)
Philipp Otto Runge, German Romantic painter, born in Wolgast, West Pomerania (d. 1810)
Joseph Hartmann Stuntz, Swiss composer, born in Arlesheim, Switzerland (d. 1859)
Franz Berwald, Swedish violinist, composer (Estrella de Soria; Sinfonie sérieuse), and orthopedist, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1868)
Johann Vesque von Püttlingen, Austrian lawyer and composer, born in Lubomirski Palace, Opole Lubelskie, Poland (d. 1883)
Eduard Marxsen, German pianist and composer, born in Nienstädten, Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1887)
Charlotte Saunders Cushman, American actress (Lady MacBeth), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1876)
Darius Nash Couch, American businessman, naturalist, and Major General (Union Army), born in (d. 1897)
Coventry Patmore, English poet and critic (The Angel in the House), born in Essex, England (d. 1896)
Gabriel C. Wharton, American civil engineer and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Culpeper County, Virginia (d. 1906)
Richard Hol, Dutch composer, organist and conductor, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1904)
Pieter Caland, Dutch hydraulic engineer (New Waterway), born in Zierikzee, Zeeland, Netherlands (d. 1902)
Jonathan Hutchinson, English surgeon and scientist, born in Selby, Yorkshire, England (d. 1913)
James Cardinal Gibbons, Archbishop of Baltimore, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1921)
Édouard Colonne, French composer and violinist, born in Bordeaux, France (d. 1910)
Edmund John Armstrong, Irish poet, born in Dublin (d. 1865)
Harriet Williams Russell Strong, American inventor, agricultural entrepreneur (created dams and  reservoirs in California), women’s rights activist, born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1926)[1]
Géza Zichy, Hungarian composer and one-armed pianist, born at Sztára Castle, Hungary  (d. 1924)
Charles Bannerman, Australian cricket batsman (3 Tests; first centurion in Test history 165* 1877), born in Woolwich, Kent, England (d. 1930)
William Gillette, American stage and silent film actor (Sherlock Holmes), and playwright (Secret Service), born in Hartford, Connecticut, (d. 1937)
Arthur H. Bird, American composer, born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1923)
Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Indian early nationalist leader, born in Ratnagiri, British India (d. 1920)
Alexander Willem Frederik Idenburg, Dutch politician of the Anti Revolutionary Party and Governor-General of Dutch Indies (1909-16), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1935)
Apolinario Mabini, Filipino political theoretician and 1st Prime Minister of the Philippines, born in Tanauan, Batangas, Philippines (d. 1903)
Friedrich Aereboe, German landlord and agricultural economist, born in Horn, Hamburg (d. 1942)
Max Heindel, Danish-American Christian occultist, astrologer and mystic, born in Aarhus, Denmark (d. 1919)
Francesco Cilea, Italian orchestral and opera composer (L'arlesiana; Adriana Lecouvreur), born in Palmi, Calabria, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1950)
William Gillies Whittaker, English composer, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1944)
Alan Brooke, 1st Viscount Alanbrooke, British field marshal, born in Bragnieres-de-Bigorre, France (d. 1963)
Albert Warner [Abraham Albert Wonsal], American film producer (Warner Bros. Studio), born in Krasnosielc, Congress Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1967)
Apolinary Szeluto, Polish pianist and composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1966)
Arthur Whitten Brown, British aviator (first nonstop airplane crossing of the Atlantic with John W. Alcock), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1948)
Jan Poortenaar, Dutch painter, etcher and cartoonist, born in Amsterdam (d. 1958)
Salvador de Madariaga y Rojo, Spanish writer (Englishmen, Frenchmen, Spaniards) and diplomat, born in La Coruña, Spain (d. 1978)
Walter H. Schottky, German physicist (Thermodynamik), born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1976)
Gluyas Williams, American cartoonist (Fellows Citizen), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1982)
Milan Stojadinović, Serbian-Yugoslav political figure and 12th Prime Minister of Yugoslavia (1935-39), born in Čačak, Kingdom of Serbia (d. 1961)
Raymond Chandler, American-British mystery novelist and screenwriter (Farewell My Lovely; The Long Goodbye; The Big Sleep), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1959)
Petros John Petridis, Greek composer (Isotope Suite), born in Nigdé (Capodocia), Turkey (d. 1977)
Karl Menninger, American psychiatrist (Menninger Clinic), born in Topeka, Kansas (d. 1990)

Arthur Treacher, British character actor (The Little Princess; Thank You, Jeeves), TV announcer/sidekick (The Merv Griffin Show, 1965-70), and fish and chips restaurant namesake, born in Brighton, England (d. 1975)
Aileen Pringle, American actress (Murder at Midnight), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1989)
Florence Vidor, American actress (Jack Knife Man), born in Houston, Texas (d. 1977)
Jacob Marschak, Ukrainian-American economist (econometrics), born in Kiev, Russia (d. 1977)
Red Dutton, Canadian ice hockey player (Calgary Tigers) and coach, born in Russell, Manitoba (d. 1987)
Gustav Heinemann, President of West Germany (1969-74), born in Schwelm, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1976)
Hank Worden, American actor (The Searcher, Red River), born in Rolfe, Iowa (d. 1992)
Walter Burle Marx, Brazilian pianist and composer, born in São Paulo, Brazil (d. 1990)
Adone Zecchi, Italian composer, born in Bologna, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1995)
Erich Itor Kahn, German composer of Jewish descent, born in Rimbach, Odenwald, German Empire (d. 1956)
Chandra Sekhar Azad, Indian revolutionary (Hindustan Socialist Republican Army), born in Bhavra, Central India Agency, British India (d. 1931)
Clancy Cooper, American actor (Enchanted Forest, Girls in Chains), born in Boise, Idaho (d. 1975)
Vladimir Prelog, Croatian-Swiss organic chemist (Nobel 1975), born in Sarajevo, Austria-Hungary (d. 1998)
Wolfgang Gentner, German nuclear physicist, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1980)
Elspeth Huxley, English author (The Flame Trees of Thika and The Mottled Lizard), born in London (d. 1997)
Karl Swenson, American actor (Lars Hanson-Little House on the Prairie), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1978)
Michael Wilding, British actor (Stage Fright, Courtney Affair, World of Suzie Wong), born in Leigh-on-Sea, Essex, England (d. 1979)
Coral Browne, Australian actress (Ruling Class, Time Express), born in Melbourne, Victoria (d. 1991)
Ernest van der Eyken, Belgian violist, composer, and conductor (Youth and Music Orchestra of Antwerp, 1963-76), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 2010)
Michael Foot, British politician (L) and writer, Leader of the Opposition (1980-83), born in Plymouth, Devon (d. 2010)
Alastair Down, Scottish CEO (Burmah Oil), born in Kirkcaldy, Fife (d. 2004)
(William Jennings Bryan) "Ben" Weber, American composer (Thorne Music Award, 1965), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1979)
Ronald Ridout, English school textbook author (English Today), born in Farnham, Surrey (d. 1994)
Charles Kerruish, Manx politician and President of Tyndwald Isle of Man, born in Isle of Man (d. 2003)
John Stokes, British MP (C), born in Sandy, Bedfordshire (d. 2003)
Kurt Kreuger, Swiss-German actor (Fear, Unfaithfully Yours), born in Michenberg, Germany (d. 2006)
Ray Scarborough, American MLB pitcher (All Star 1950), born in Mount Gilead, North Carolina (d. 1982)
Harold "Pee Wee" Reese, American Baseball HOF shortstop (10 x MLB All-Star; World Series 1955, 59 Brooklyn/LA Dodgers), born in Ekron, Kentucky (d. 1999)
Amália Rodrigues, Portuguese fado singer, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1999)
Marcel Maeyer, Belgian sculptor and graphic artist, born in Sint-Niklaas, Belgium (d. 1993)
Calvert DeForest, American comedian (Larry "Bud" Melman), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2007)
Harry Hookway, English first Chief Executive of the British Library (1973-84), born in London, England (d. 2014)
Heinz Bennent, German actor (Possession; The Serpent's Egg), born in Stolberg, Germany (d. 2011)
Jerome Rosen, American composer and educator, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2011)
Peter Twiss, British test pilot (breaking the World Air Speed Record and being the first person to exceed 1,000 mph in flight), born in Lindfield, Sussex (d. 2011)
Moses Rosen, Chief Rabbi of Romania (1948-94), born in Moineşti, Bacău, Romania (d. 1994)
Stefans Grové, South African composer (Nonyana, The Ceremonial Dancer; Cyclops; Glimpses), born in Bethlehem, Orange Free State, Union of South Africa (d. 2014)
Cyril M. Kornbluth, American sci-fi writer (Space Merchants), born in New York City (d. 1958)
Luis Aloma, Cuban baseball player, born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1997)
Betsy Haworth, Church of England deaconess and minister, born in Leigh, Lancashire (d. 2007)
Gavin Lambert, British-American screenwriter (Inside Daisy Clover), born in East Grinstead, England (d. 2005)
Mortimer Matz, American public relations expert and co-founder of Nathan's Famous Hot Dog Eating Contest, born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2025)
Gloria DeHaven, American musical actress (Step Lively), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2016)
Pierre Baugniet, Belgian pairs ice skater (Olympic gold 1948), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1981)
Ludvik Vaculik, Czech writer (Two Thousand Words), born in Brumo, Zlín Region (d. 2015)
Al McCandless, American Republican politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from California), born in Brawley, California (d. 2017)
Elliot McKay See Jr., American engineer and NASA astronaut, born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1966)
Bill Lee, American session double-bassist, and film score composer (Do The Right Thing; She's Gotta Have It), born in Snow Hill, Alabama (d. 2023)
Cy Young, American athlete and only US male to win gold in javelin throwing (1952 Olympics), born in Modesto, California (d. 2017)
Hubert Selby Jr., American author (Last Exit to Brooklyn), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2004)
Leon Fleisher, American pianist and conductor (Annapolis Symphony, 1970-82), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2020)[1]
Vera Rubin, American astronomer who discovered dark matter, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2016)
Andrew Rutherford, Scottish scholar and Vice-Chancellor of the University of London (1994-97), born in Helmsdale, Sutherland, Scotland (d. 1998)
Billy Maxwell, American golfer (US Amateur C'ship 1951; Ryder Cup 1963; 7 x PGA Tour wins), born in Abilene, Texas (d. 2021)
Danny Barcelona, Filipino-American jazz drummer (Louis Armstrong's All-Stars), born in Waipahu, Territory of Hawaii (d. 2007)
Jack Richardson, Canadian record producer (The Guess Who), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2011)
Roger Hassenforder, French racing cyclist (8 stage wins Tour de France 1955-59), born in Sausheim, France (d. 2021)
Arata Isozaki, Japanese architect (Pritzker Prize, 2019; Art Tower Mito, Los Angeles Museum of Contemporary Art; Walt Disney headquarters), born in Oito, Japan (d. 2022)[1]
Guy Fournier, French Canadian author and screenwriter, born in Waterloo, Quebec
